Logic & Critical Thinking: A company says its product is best because it is the market leader. Is that real evidence or a weak argument?
A friend says one bad teacher proves the whole school is failing. What is the weak leap?
A coworker attacks a person's attitude instead of answering their point. How would you refocus the argument?
A review says a restaurant is terrible after one slow night. What assumption should be questioned?
A politician says a plan worked because crime dropped afterward. What else would you check?
Someone says you must support their idea or you hate progress. What false choice is being used?
A student says homework is useless because one assignment was pointless. What evidence is missing?
An ad says thousands of people bought the product, so it must be good. What does popularity fail to prove?
A manager says a worker is unreliable because they were late once. What would make that conclusion fair or unfair?
A friend says, 'It happened after I warned you, so I caused it.' What reasoning mistake might be happening?
Epistemology: Two doctors give different advice based on different studies. What would help you decide which opinion to trust?
A friend sends a viral video with no source. What would you check before believing it?
A family story has been repeated for years. What evidence would help you know if it is accurate?
A podcast guest says they did their own research. What would make their conclusion trustworthy?
A photo seems to prove a claim, but you do not know when it was taken. What should you verify?
Someone says they saw it with their own eyes. When can eyewitness evidence still be wrong?
A poll says most people agree. What does that prove, and what does it not prove?
An expert changes their recommendation after new data. When should that increase your trust?
A website looks professional but lists no author. How much should design affect your trust?
A belief has helped you for years. What kind of evidence would justify questioning it?
Ethics & Moral Reasoning: A manager can save money by cutting hours for part-time workers. What should they consider before deciding?
A student finds answers to tomorrow's test online. What makes using them wrong, risky, or defensible?
A company can use cheaper materials that are legal but less safe. What should guide the decision?
Your friend asks you to lie so they avoid consequences. What does loyalty require here?
A city can help thousands but will damage one small neighborhood. What should be weighed before approving it?
A nurse has limited time and two patients need help. What is the fairest way to decide who comes first?
A business owner discovers a pricing mistake that benefits them. What should they do before keeping the money?
A school punishes a whole class because no one admits who broke the rule. Is that fair?
A family member needs money, but helping them may hurt your own household. What responsibility comes first?
A leader hides bad news to prevent panic. When does protecting people become misleading them?
Strategic Thinking: A company cuts employee training to improve quarterly profit. What might happen over the next three years?
A student takes an easy class to protect their GPA. What could that choice cost later?
A business lowers prices to beat a competitor. What could happen if customers expect discounts forever?
A leader wins an argument publicly but embarrasses a teammate. What problem might appear next?
A family buys a bigger house because rates are low. What risks should they think through first?
A startup hires fast to impress investors. What could go wrong if the culture is not ready?
A city delays road repairs to save money this year. What future costs might grow?
A manager avoids a hard conversation to keep peace. What could that silence cost the team later?
A teenager posts a harsh joke to get attention. What second-order effects could follow?
A store sells a popular product at a loss to bring people in. What must be true for that strategy to work?
Street Lessons: If someone only calls when they need something, are they a friend or are you a resource?
A coworker asks you to sign off on work you did not do because everyone does it. What should you consider before saying yes?
Someone keeps complimenting you before asking for a big favor. What possibilities should you consider?
A friend asks to borrow money but has not paid back others. What should you think through before saying yes?
A person gets angry when you set a boundary. What might that reveal about the relationship?
Someone offers you a quick way to make money but avoids details. What should you watch for?
A group pressures you to react publicly to an insult. When is silence the stronger move?
A new friend shares private gossip about others. What does that suggest they may do with your business later?
A boss says, 'Trust me,' but will not put the agreement in writing. What is the risk?
Someone wants your loyalty before they have shown character. What would you need to see first?
Financial Judgment: You receive a $10,000 bonus. What would help you decide whether to pay debt, invest, or keep cash?
A store offers zero-interest financing if you buy today. What should you check before agreeing?
A friend says a stock is guaranteed to go up. What questions should you ask before investing?
A family wants a bigger car payment because the monthly cost seems manageable. What else matters?
Someone wants to use a credit card for a vacation they cannot afford now. What is the real cost?
A worker gets a raise and immediately upgrades their lifestyle. What risk does that create?
A couple has no emergency fund but wants to invest aggressively. What tradeoff should they consider?
A person chooses the cheapest insurance plan. What could make that choice expensive later?
A small business owner discounts heavily to get customers. What should they know about margins?
A retiree considers helping an adult child financially. What should they protect before saying yes?
Leadership & Influence: A team member keeps missing deadlines but is well-liked. How should a leader handle it without damaging trust?
A manager takes credit for a team's work in front of executives. What does that do to influence?
A coach yells to motivate players, but some shut down. What should the coach consider?
A leader promises change but never follows up. What happens to trust over time?
An employee makes a serious mistake and admits it quickly. How should a leader respond?
A team is confused because priorities keep changing. What should the leader clarify first?
A popular employee breaks a rule that others follow. What should accountability look like?
A leader wants buy-in for a hard decision. What should they explain before asking people to commit?
A supervisor avoids feedback because they want to be liked. What does that cost the team?
A new leader inherits a team that distrusts management. What first move could rebuild credibility?
Media & Information Literacy: Two news outlets cover the same protest but show different footage. What should you ask before deciding what happened?
A headline says a celebrity destroyed a company, but the article is mostly opinion. What should you separate?
A viral post uses one shocking statistic with no source. What should you check before sharing it?
A video clip starts after an argument already began. What context might be missing?
An influencer reviews a product but may be paid by the brand. What should viewers know?
A news story quotes only people who agree. What would make the coverage stronger?
A chart looks dramatic because the scale starts at 90. What is the visual trying to do?
A politician's speech is summarized by a hostile account. What should you watch or read before reacting?
A social media trend says everyone is angry about an issue. What might the algorithm be amplifying?
A documentary tells a powerful story but leaves out opposing evidence. What should you look for next?
Science & Evidence: A headline says people who drink coffee live longer. What would you ask before assuming coffee caused it?
A supplement company cites one small study. What would make the evidence stronger?
A school says test scores improved after a new program. What else could explain the change?
A weather forecast says there is a 70% chance of rain. What does that actually mean for your plans?
A study uses only 20 people. What limits does that create?
A medical trial shows benefits but also side effects. What should patients compare?
A nutrition video says one food causes weight gain. What evidence would you need?
A company tests its own product and reports great results. What bias should you consider?
A lab result changes after a second test. Why can repeating a test matter?
A study finds a link between screen time and anxiety. What would show whether one causes the other?
History & Civilization: During the Cuban Missile Crisis, both sides could have escalated. What choices helped avoid disaster?
Kodak invented digital photography but stayed attached to film. What historical pattern does that show?
After Rome weakened, some blamed leaders while others blamed institutions. What evidence would you compare?
The Berlin Airlift avoided direct war while resisting pressure. What can leaders learn from that?
The Challenger disaster followed ignored warnings. What does it teach about institutions under pressure?
The Marshall Plan rebuilt economies after war. Why might helping former enemies be strategic?
Civil Rights leaders used disciplined protest against unfair laws. What made that strategy powerful?
The Industrial Revolution created wealth and hardship. What should be judged from both sides?
A country writes history after winning a war. What voices might be missing?
Apollo 13 survived because teams adapted under pressure. What does that reveal about preparation?
Technology & AI: A school allows AI for homework but not tests. What problems could that solve and create?
A company uses AI to screen resumes. What risks should applicants and managers consider?
A teenager trusts a chatbot for mental health advice. What boundaries should exist?
A deepfake video of a public figure spreads online. What should people verify before reacting?
An app offers convenience in exchange for location data. What should users weigh?
A warehouse automates jobs to lower costs. What responsibilities does the company have to workers?
A social media feed learns what keeps you watching. What incentive does the algorithm have?
A hospital uses AI to flag high-risk patients. What should humans still review?
A driverless car must choose between safety risks. Who should set the rules?
A student uses AI to write an essay but learns little. What is the hidden cost?
Creativity & Innovation: A restaurant is losing lunch customers. Instead of lowering prices, what creative change could it test?
A teacher has bored students and no extra budget. What could they change about the experience?
A small store cannot compete with Amazon on price. What could it offer that Amazon cannot?
A city park is underused. What low-cost experiment could bring people back?
A startup's first product fails. What should the team learn before building the next version?
A family wants to save money but hates strict budgets. What creative system could they try?
A musician cannot get attention online. What constraint could force a more original approach?
A company gets the same customer complaint every week. How could it turn the complaint into an idea?
A student struggles to study with long notes. What different format could they test?
A nonprofit has few volunteers. What could make helping easier or more rewarding?
Work, Purpose & Ambition: Someone hates their job but has no savings. What should they think through before quitting?
A student picks a major only because it sounds prestigious. What should they consider?
A worker wants a raise but has not tracked results. What should they prepare first?
A person starts a side business while exhausted from work. What tradeoff should they watch?
A teenager wants success but avoids boring practice. What does ambition require here?
A manager offers a promotion with more stress and little pay increase. What should be weighed?
A person changes careers after one bad month. What would show whether it is a pattern or a rough season?
A business owner works nonstop but ignores family and health. What could success cost?
A college graduate compares their progress to friends online. What should they measure instead?
A skilled worker refuses to learn new tools. What risk does that create over time?
Identity & Human Behavior: Someone gets defensive every time they receive feedback. What might be happening beneath that reaction?
A person keeps choosing the same unhealthy relationship pattern. What should they notice?
A student says they are bad at math after one failed test. How can identity trap them?
A parent loses patience after work and regrets it later. What pattern should they study?
A person buys things to feel successful. What need might the habit be serving?
A friend always assumes criticism is disrespect. What belief could be shaping that response?
Someone avoids trying because failure would hurt their self-image. What is the hidden cost?
A person changes their opinion to fit every group. What might they be protecting?
A worker procrastinates only on important tasks. What fear might be involved?
A person apologizes quickly but repeats the behavior. What should change besides the words?
Literature & Timeless Wisdom: In many stories, a character gets what they want but loses what matters. What real-life warning does that reveal?
In The Great Gatsby, status does not bring peace. What modern ambition can fall into the same trap?
Aesop's tortoise beats the hare through consistency. Where does slow discipline beat talent in real life?
In The Odyssey, distractions delay the journey home. What pulls people away from their real goals today?
Shakespeare often shows pride turning into downfall. When does confidence become dangerous?
The Prince focuses on power and perception. What should modern leaders learn without becoming cynical?
To Kill a Mockingbird shows courage under social pressure. What does quiet courage look like today?
The Art of War values winning without unnecessary conflict. When is avoiding a fight the smarter victory?
The Intelligent Investor warns against emotion in markets. Where else do feelings distort judgment?
A proverb says, 'Measure twice, cut once.' What decision in life deserves that kind of patience?
